questions.question: During heat-treat verification, a 7075 aluminum fitting expected to read about 32% IACS in the T6 condition instead measures about 40% IACS. The instrument was standardized on bracketing standards and all temperatures were equalized. The most likely explanation is:
options: The fitting is harder and stronger than the T6 requirement
The fitting is overaged (a T73-type condition) or has suffered heat damage — conditions that raise conductivity above the T6 value
The fitting has elevated magnetic permeability from machining stress
The reading is normal — 7075-T6 routinely reads anywhere from 30% to 45% IACS
questions.explanation: Temper sets conductivity in aluminum alloys: 7075-T6 reads about 32% IACS while overaged 7075-T73 reads about 40%, and overheating or fire exposure drives readings upward toward the overaged and annealed conditions. Higher conductivity here signals a softer, lower-strength state — not extra hardness — and 7075 is nonmagnetic, so permeability is not in play.

questions.question: On the normalized impedance plane, copper tested at 5 kHz can land at roughly the same operating point that Ti-6Al-4V occupies at 500 kHz. This is possible because position along the conductivity curve is set by:
options: the conductivity of the part alone, so matching points indicate an instrument fault
the instrument's gain and display sensitivity settings
the coil's own winding resistance
the product of frequency, conductivity, and permeability — not conductivity alone
questions.explanation: The operating point follows f × σ × μ, so raising frequency slides every alloy farther down the same comma-shaped curve. A low-conductivity alloy at high frequency can occupy the spot a high-conductivity alloy holds at low frequency — which is why material identity comes from position plus the test settings, never position alone.

questions.question: A mill runs eddy current examination on steel product while it is still above about 770 °C, with no magnetic saturation equipment at that station. Saturation is unnecessary there because:
options: eddy currents cannot be induced in hot metal, so only geometry is being measured
the heat raises the steel's conductivity until it behaves like copper
above the Curie temperature, ferromagnetism vanishes and the steel responds like a nonmagnetic conductor
high temperature drives the steel's permeability even higher, which improves penetration
questions.explanation: Above the Curie point (about 770 °C for iron) the material loses its ferromagnetism, so there is no permeability noise to suppress — the hot product tests electromagnetically like a nonmagnetic metal. Heating actually lowers conductivity, and high permeability would collapse penetration, not improve it.

questions.question: With identical probe setups, a small machined bracket and an entire wing spar of the same alloy give the same impedance reading. This is expected because the 'mass' that affects coil impedance is:
options: the amount of conductive material inside the coil's field — a footprint-sized ring reaching to about 3δ — so identical local sections look identical
the total weight of the part, so matching readings indicate a standardization error
proportional to the part's overall surface area
the alloy's density multiplied by the part's total volume
questions.explanation: The coil samples only the volume its field links: a ring about the size of the windings reaching roughly three standard depths of penetration into the part. If the section inside that sensing volume is the same, a bracket and a wing spar are indistinguishable — metal outside the field contributes nothing.

questions.question: A low-frequency corrosion survey is set up so the aluminum skin is thinner than the 3δ effective depth. As the wall progressively thins, the operating point on the impedance plane:
options: moves farther down the conductivity curve, away from the air point
rises above the air point along the permeability direction
remains stationary while only the signal amplitude changes
walks along a curling thickness locus back toward the air point — zero metal would be an empty coil
questions.explanation: With t < 3δ the exponential decay of eddy currents is truncated, so every increment of metal loss reduces the loading on the coil and the point unwinds from the thick-part position back toward the air point. Calibrated against steps of known thickness, that path is the basis of eddy current thinning gauges.

questions.question: Under the law of similarity, an encircling-coil technique proven on one diameter of nonmagnetic rod reproduces the same operating point — and geometrically similar signals — on a different diameter when:
options: the f/fg ratio and the fill factor are held the same, recomputing the test frequency for the new diameter
the same test frequency is used, since the alloy has not changed
the drive voltage is scaled in proportion to the diameter change
the gain is raised until signal amplitudes match the original setup
questions.explanation: The normalized operating point is fixed by the ratio f/fg, where fg = 5066/(σ × μr × d²); since fg falls with the square of diameter, the test frequency must be recomputed to hold the ratio. Equal f/fg plus equal fill factor reproduces the point — drive and gain change amplitude, never position.

questions.question: On a nonmagnetic part, signals are weak relative to the instrument's electronic noise. Compared with raising the instrument gain, raising the coil drive level:
options: increases the depth of penetration, which gain cannot do
moves the normalized operating point farther down the conductivity curve
strengthens the field and the eddy currents themselves, gaining signal over the electronic noise floor — whereas gain amplifies signal and electronic noise together
has an identical effect — both act on the signal after the coil
questions.explanation: Drive raises the physics: more ampere-turns means stronger eddy currents and bigger signals before the amplifier, buying real margin over electronic noise; gain works after the coil and lifts electronic noise by the same factor as the signal. Neither moves the normalized operating point on a nonmagnetic part — that is set by frequency, conductivity, permeability, and geometry — and material noise grows with drive just as the flaw signal does.

questions.question: A crack-detection procedure for painted aluminum structure requires the reference-standard sensitivity check to be performed through the maximum coating thickness the procedure allows. The technical basis is that:
options: nonconductive paint chemically absorbs the electromagnetic field energy
coupling loss is sensitivity loss — lift-off weakens every signal because less field reaches the metal, so a crack under the thickest permitted coating answers weakest
thick coatings rotate crack signals into the lift-off direction, making them unreadable
coatings raise the surface conductivity of the part, masking crack responses
questions.explanation: Lift-off does not merely write its own trace — it reduces the field reaching the part, so every other signal, crack responses included, shrinks as coating thickens. Demonstrating detection through the worst-case permitted coating proves the technique still finds the required flaw under the least favorable coupling.

questions.question: A surface probe performs normally on its standard cable but becomes unusable near the top of its marked frequency range after a long extension cable is added. The most likely cause is:
options: the longer cable's resistance has cut the coil drive current in half
the extension cable is acting as an antenna for shop electrical noise
the added cable capacitance has dragged the probe's self-resonant frequency down toward the test frequency
the extension cable reduces the probe's fill factor
questions.explanation: The winding's distributed capacitance plus the cable's capacitance forms a resonant circuit with the coil inductance L₀, and a probe is usable only below that self-resonant frequency. Extra cable adds capacitance and pulls the resonance down — the reason probes carry marked frequency ranges and cable changes can invalidate a setup.

questions.question: Hunting a flaw 10 mm deep in aluminum, a technician lowers the test frequency until the calculated effective depth (3δ) extends well past 10 mm — yet the 3 mm pencil probe still detects nothing. The reason is that:
options: the coil's axial field dies out within a distance on the order of the coil radius — a separate ceiling from skin depth — so the small probe's field never reaches the flaw
the skin-depth formula does not apply at frequencies this low
eddy currents cannot be generated at frequencies low enough to penetrate 10 mm
aluminum's permeability blocks the field below the surface
questions.explanation: Depth of interrogation has two ceilings: the skin-depth limit set by frequency, conductivity, and permeability, and a geometric limit — the axial field of a surface coil decays within roughly one coil radius. Whichever ceiling is lower wins, so deep-penetration probes must be physically large as well as low-frequency; a 3 mm probe cannot reach 10 mm at any frequency.
